posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
2007 Joh. Jos. Prüm Wehlener Sonnenuhr Riesling Spätlese - Germany, Mosel Saar Ruwer (5/1/2016)
– popped and poured –
– tasted non-blind over a few hours –

NOSE: moderately expressive; medium petrol note; hint of raspberry; typical Prum W-S nose.

BODY: no spritz; pale yellow color; medium bodied.

TASTE: seems to present as a proper Spatlese; 8.5% alc. not noticeable; medium acidity; fruit flavors of gala apple, pear; and green grapes; strong mineral aspect o nthe finish, which is long and of moderate intensity; background hint of raspberry; seems a bit hollow on the mid-palate – maybe at an awkward age right now?; firmly beyond infancy. Hold.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Brian G r a f s t r o m:

maybe at an awkward age right now?

I think you’re correct - but it’s still damn good. I guess awkward is relative to how high the potential is.

Brian G r a f s t r o m:

Hold.

…or drink! …or better yet, buy a couple cases and try one every few months to follow the evolution. That’s what we’ve been doing with the 2007 Graacher Himmelreich Spatlese.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
I bought 11 bottles of this wine (over a couple of purchases). That’s the most I’ve ever purchased of any non-daily drinker wine. I feel head over heels for this wine upon release, but the last couple showings (including one a year ago) weren’t as inspiring. That said, it’s still very good QPR, and there is plenty of track record to support one’s hope for a brighter future.

I only have one bottle of the 2007 Graacher Himmelreich Spatlese, so you’ll have to tell me when to drink it!
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Russ Williams
posts.cooked:
Brian G r a f s t r o m:

I only have one bottle of the 2007 Graacher Himmelreich Spatlese, so you’ll have to tell me when to drink it!

I had an ‘01 last fall that was friggin’ awesome. I’m buying a lot of the '15. I could actually see buying a case of this, the WS Spatlese and Auslese.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Brian G r a f s t r o m:

I only have one bottle of the 2007 Graacher Himmelreich Spatlese, so you’ll have to tell me when to drink it!

I’ll be sure to update you as events warrant.

Russ Williams:

I had an ‘01 last fall that was friggin’ awesome. I’m buying a lot of the '15. I could actually see buying a case of this, the WS Spatlese and Auslese.

My wife and I actually prefer G-H to W-S. The ‘01 G-H Spatlese and Auslese are both friggin’ awesome! If you get lucky, you can find the '02 G-H and W-S markedly cheaper than their '01 counterparts with only a small drop off in quality. Plus the '02 is further along in it’s evolution than the '01. Gotta love German consistency!

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked:
Dennis Kanagie:

My wife and I actually prefer G-H to W-S. The ‘01 G-H Spatlese and Auslese are both friggin’ awesome! If you get lucky, you can find the '02 G-H and W-S markedly cheaper than their '01 counterparts with only a small drop off in quality. Plus the '02 is further along in it’s evolution than the '01. Gotta love German consistency!

I’m also a big fan of the Himmelreich. How would you compare the Himmelreich and Sonnenuhr in terms of style?

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
David_K:

I’m also a big fan of the Himmelreich. How would you compare the Himmelreich and Sonnenuhr in terms of style?

The G-H seems to be more fruity with less minerality / slate than the W-S. While the W-S is the technically superior Riesling and seems to age better, we find the G-H more fun to drink, especially young. All in all, there’s not a huge difference in quality or style between the two bottlings. One thing we’ve noticed is the W-S has a longer dormancy than the G-H, but neither shut down completely. The differences are subtle and would make for a good lesson in terroir.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I had the 2007 Wehlener Sonnenuhr Kabinett a few days ago. Also too young, but at least expressive, pleasurable and clearly very good.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: One of the signature elements I tend to find in many if not all Wehlener Sonnenuhr Rieslings is a suggestion of lemon cream. It’s a marker that tips me off to the site in wines from multiple producers.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
D@vid Bu3ker:

One of the signature elements I tend to find in many if not all Wehlener Sonnenuhr Rieslings is a suggestion of lemon cream.

Agreed - it does have a cream thing going on. Nice way to describe it.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
2007 Joh. Jos. Prüm Wehlener Sonnenuhr Riesling Spätlese - Germany, Mosel Saar Ruwer (5/2/2024)
– popped and poured –
– tasted non-blind over 2 - 3 hrs. –
– AP No.: … 11 08 –

NOSE: “green” petrol; papaya and concentrated watermelon; expressive.

BODY: medium gold color; full bodied.

TASTE: mango; low acid; candied ginger; dried apricot; 8.5% alc. hidden; poooooooosssssibly a touch of botrytis. Quite tasty, for sure, but I’m concerned with the low acidity — this might be a WS Spat. to drink earlier than is typical — if not that, maybe I’ve caught this at an awkward in-between stage — five more bottles await in the cellar, so I’ll eventually find out. Drink or Hold.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m
